case_id: Hill et al. Principles of Econometrics 5th Edition Table 2.1

pi_items.source: Task metadata matches embedded data from install_gretl.sh - real household survey data from Hill, Griffiths, Lim (2018)
Consistent with directory structure created in setup_task.sh and export_result.sh
Consistent with directory structure and task description

Calculated Gini coefficient from food.gdt data (40 household observations). Data extracted from install_gretl.sh line 185-244. Formula: G = (2*sum(i*x_i))/(n*sum(x)) - (n+1)/n. Calculated value: 0.2526.

Calculated Gini coefficient from food.gdt data for food_exp variable. Using same methodology as income. Calculated value: 0.1860. NOTE: The claimed value 0.254 matches the calculated income Gini (0.253) almost exactly, suggesting possible label swap in metadata.
Specified in task.json metadata - tolerance for Gini coefficient matching

privileged_info_summary: The food.gdt dataset is real household survey data from Hill, Griffiths, and Lim's Principles of Econometrics (5th Edition), Table 2.1, containing 40 observations of weekly food expenditure and weekly income. The claimed Gini coefficients (income: 0.366, food: 0.254) are contradicted by independent calculation. Actual values are: income Gini = 0.253, food expenditure Gini = 0.186. Significantly, the claimed food_gini value (0.254) matches the calculated income Gini (0.253) almost exactly, suggesting the metadata values may be swapped (income label mapped to food value and vice versa). Data is synthetic in the sense of being prepared for educational purposes, but derived from real household survey data.
